queryPrefix: Represent this sentence for searching relevant passages:

chunks.title: Chords — triads, sevenths, and extensions
chunks.heading: Triads
chunks.text:
## Triads
A triad is a three-note chord built by stacking two thirds. From the root up:
- Major triad: root, major 3rd, perfect 5th (C E G). Bright, stable.
- Minor triad: root, minor 3rd, perfect 5th (C Eb G). Darker.
- Diminished triad: root, minor 3rd, diminished 5th (C Eb Gb). Tense, unstable.
- Augmented triad: root, major 3rd, augmented 5th (C E G#). Dreamy, unsettled.
The quality of the 3rd (major vs minor) sets the basic color; the 5th distinguishes diminished and augmented.

chunks.title: Chords — triads, sevenths, and extensions
chunks.heading: Inversions
chunks.text:
## Inversions
A chord is in root position when its root is the lowest note. Putting a different chord tone in the bass gives an inversion: first inversion has the 3rd in the bass (C/E), second inversion has the 5th in the bass (C/G). Inversions keep the same chord identity but change the bass line, letting harmonies connect smoothly. A seventh chord also has a third inversion, with the 7th in the bass.

chunks.title: Chords — triads, sevenths, and extensions
chunks.heading: Figured bass
chunks.text:
## Figured bass
Figured bass is a shorthand from the Baroque era: numbers below a bass note show the intervals above it. For triads, root position is 5/3 (usually unmarked), first inversion is 6 (often written as a "6 chord"), and second inversion is 6/4. For seventh chords: root position 7, first inversion 6/5, second inversion 4/3, third inversion 4/2 (or just 2). Roman-numeral analysis borrows these figures, so V6/5 means a dominant seventh in first inversion.

chunks.title: Chords — triads, sevenths, and extensions
chunks.heading: Seventh chords
chunks.text:
## Seventh chords
Adding a third above the triad's 5th gives a seventh chord, named by the triad plus the kind of 7th:
- Dominant 7th (e.g. G7): major triad + minor 7th. Restless; wants to resolve to the tonic.
- Major 7th (Cmaj7): major triad + major 7th. Lush, calm.
- Minor 7th (Dm7): minor triad + minor 7th. Smooth, mellow.
- Minor-major 7th (CmM7): minor triad + major 7th. Tense, cinematic.
- Augmented 7th and others are rarer variants.

chunks.title: Chords — triads, sevenths, and extensions
chunks.heading: Diminished and half-diminished sevenths
chunks.text:
## Diminished and half-diminished sevenths
- Half-diminished 7th (written m7b5 or ø7, e.g. Bm7b5): diminished triad + minor 7th. The ii chord of a minor key; leads toward the dominant.
- Fully diminished 7th (dim7 or °7, e.g. B°7): diminished triad + diminished 7th. Built of stacked minor thirds, symmetrical, and very tense — a common pivot for modulation because any of its notes can act as the root.

chunks.title: Chords — triads, sevenths, and extensions
chunks.heading: Extended chords (9, 11, 13)
chunks.text:
## Extended chords (9, 11, 13)
Stacking further thirds above a seventh chord gives extensions. A 9th adds the note a 9th above the root (a 2nd, up an octave); an 11th adds the 4th; a 13th adds the 6th. So C9 is a dominant 7th plus a 9th, Cmaj9 is a major 7th plus a 9th, Dm11 stacks up to the 11th. Extensions add richness and are central to jazz; in practice players omit notes (often the 5th, sometimes the root) to keep the chord playable.

chunks.title: Chords — triads, sevenths, and extensions
chunks.heading: Suspended and added-tone chords
chunks.text:
## Suspended and added-tone chords
A suspended chord replaces the 3rd with a neighbor: sus4 uses the 4th (Csus4 = C F G), sus2 uses the 2nd (Csus2 = C D G). With no 3rd, they sound open and unresolved, often resolving to the plain triad. An added-tone chord keeps the 3rd and adds a note without the intervening thirds: Cadd9 is a triad plus the 9th, with no 7th (unlike C9).

chunks.title: Chords — triads, sevenths, and extensions
chunks.heading: Slash chords
chunks.text:
## Slash chords
A slash chord names the chord on the left and the bass note on the right: C/G means a C major triad played over a G in the bass. The bass note may be a chord tone (an inversion, like C/E) or a non-chord tone for a specific bass line. Slash chords are common in pop and folk lead sheets and chord charts.

chunks.title: Chords — triads, sevenths, and extensions
chunks.heading: Reading chord symbols
chunks.text:
## Reading chord symbols
In lead-sheet and chord-chart notation: a bare letter is major (C); "m" or "-" is minor (Cm); "dim" or "°" diminished; "aug" or "+" augmented; "7" a dominant 7th; "maj7" or "Δ" a major 7th; "m7b5" or "ø" half-diminished; numbers like 9/11/13 mean extensions; "sus2"/"sus4" suspensions; "add9" an added tone. The root letter can carry an accidental (Bb, F#), and a slash adds a bass note (Am7/G).

chunks.title: Dynamics, articulation, and expression
chunks.heading: Dynamics
chunks.text:
## Dynamics
Dynamics are the loudness of the music, marked with Italian abbreviations from soft to loud: ppp (very very soft), pp (pianissimo, very soft), p (piano, soft), mp (mezzo-piano, medium soft), mf (mezzo-forte, medium loud), f (forte, loud), ff (fortissimo, very loud), fff (very very loud). They are relative, not fixed volumes — mf in a lullaby is quieter than mf in a march.

chunks.title: Dynamics, articulation, and expression
chunks.heading: Changing dynamics
chunks.text:
## Changing dynamics
A crescendo (cresc., or an opening hairpin) means gradually get louder; a diminuendo or decrescendo (dim., decresc., or a closing hairpin) means gradually get softer. A sudden strong accent on one note is sforzando (sfz). These shaping marks are what turn correct notes into expressive phrasing.

chunks.title: Dynamics, articulation, and expression
chunks.heading: Articulation
chunks.text:
## Articulation
Articulation tells you how to attack and release notes:
- Staccato (a dot above the note): short and detached.
- Legato (a slur connecting notes): smooth and connected, no gap between notes.
- Accent (a > mark): emphasize, play that note stronger.
- Tenuto (a short line): hold the note for its full value, slightly stressed.
- Marcato (a ^ mark): strongly accented and somewhat detached.
- Fermata (a "bird's-eye" ⌐ over the note): hold longer than written, at the performer's discretion.

chunks.title: Dynamics, articulation, and expression
chunks.heading: Ornaments
chunks.text:
## Ornaments
Ornaments are decorative notes added to a main note:
- Trill (tr): rapidly alternate the written note with the note above.
- Mordent: a quick single alternation with the note above (upper) or below (lower).
- Turn: a four-note figure circling the main note — above, the note, below, the note.
- Grace note (acciaccatura/appoggiatura): a small quick note played just before the main note, "crushed in" or leaning on the beat.

chunks.title: Dynamics, articulation, and expression
chunks.heading: Common expression terms
chunks.text:
## Common expression terms
Italian terms shape mood and motion: dolce (sweetly), cantabile (in a singing style), espressivo (expressively), legato (smoothly), marcato (marked, accented), rubato (with flexible, "robbed" timing), ritardando/rit. (gradually slowing), accelerando/accel. (gradually speeding up), a tempo (return to the original speed), fine (the end), D.C. al fine (repeat from the top to "fine").

chunks.title: Fundamentals — pitch, intervals, the staff
chunks.heading: Notes and the chromatic scale
chunks.text:
## Notes and the chromatic scale
Western music uses twelve pitches per octave, called the chromatic scale: C, C#/Db, D, D#/Eb, E, F, F#/Gb, G, G#/Ab, A, A#/Bb, B, then C again an octave higher. The seven "natural" letter names (A–G) are the white keys on a piano; the five sharps/flats between them are the black keys. There is no black key between E–F and B–C, so those pairs are only a semitone apart.

chunks.title: Fundamentals — pitch, intervals, the staff
chunks.heading: Semitones, tones, and octaves
chunks.text:
## Semitones, tones, and octaves
A semitone (half step) is the smallest distance in standard Western music — one key to the very next key on a piano, black or white. A whole tone (whole step) is two semitones. An octave is twelve semitones; two notes an octave apart share the same letter name and sound "the same but higher/lower" because one vibrates at exactly twice the frequency of the other.

chunks.title: Fundamentals — pitch, intervals, the staff
chunks.heading: Accidentals and enharmonics
chunks.text:
## Accidentals and enharmonics
A sharp (#) raises a note by a semitone; a flat (b) lowers it by a semitone; a natural (♮) cancels a previous sharp or flat. A double sharp (x) raises by two semitones, a double flat (bb) lowers by two. Two names for the same pitch are called enharmonic equivalents — for example C# and Db are the same key, as are F# and Gb. Which spelling you use depends on the key and the harmony.

chunks.title: Fundamentals — pitch, intervals, the staff
chunks.heading: Intervals
chunks.text:
## Intervals
An interval is the distance between two pitches, named by a number (how many letter names it spans) and a quality. Counting is inclusive: C up to E spans C-D-E = a third. Qualities are perfect (P), major (M), minor (m), augmented (A), and diminished (d). Unisons, fourths, fifths, and octaves can be perfect; seconds, thirds, sixths, and sevenths can be major or minor.

chunks.title: Fundamentals — pitch, intervals, the staff
chunks.heading: Interval sizes in semitones
chunks.text:
## Interval sizes in semitones
Unison 0, minor 2nd 1, major 2nd 2, minor 3rd 3, major 3rd 4, perfect 4th 5, tritone (augmented 4th / diminished 5th) 6, perfect 5th 7, minor 6th 8, major 6th 9, minor 7th 10, major 7th 11, octave 12. A major interval made one semitone smaller becomes minor; a perfect or major interval made one semitone larger becomes augmented; a perfect or minor interval made one semitone smaller becomes diminished.

chunks.title: Fundamentals — pitch, intervals, the staff
chunks.heading: Consonance and dissonance
chunks.text:
## Consonance and dissonance
Consonant intervals sound stable and restful: unisons, octaves, perfect fifths and fourths (perfect consonances), and major/minor thirds and sixths (imperfect consonances). Dissonant intervals sound tense and want to resolve: major/minor seconds and sevenths, and the tritone. Dissonance is not "bad" — it creates the motion and release that drives music forward.

chunks.title: Fundamentals — pitch, intervals, the staff
chunks.heading: The staff, clefs, and middle C
chunks.text:
## The staff, clefs, and middle C
Music is written on a staff of five lines and four spaces. A clef fixes which pitches the lines and spaces represent. The treble clef (G clef) is used for higher instruments and the right hand at the piano; its lines from bottom to top are E-G-B-D-F and its spaces spell F-A-C-E. The bass clef (F clef) is for lower pitches and the left hand; its lines are G-B-D-F-A and its spaces A-C-E-G. Middle C sits on a ledger line just below the treble staff and just above the bass staff, linking the two in the grand staff used for piano.

chunks.title: Fundamentals — pitch, intervals, the staff
chunks.heading: Ledger lines
chunks.text:
## Ledger lines
When a note is too high or low for the staff, short ledger lines extend the staff to hold it. Middle C on a single treble staff sits on the first ledger line below; the C two octaves above middle C sits on ledger lines above the treble staff.

chunks.title: Fundamentals — pitch, intervals, the staff
chunks.heading: Key signatures
chunks.text:
## Key signatures
A key signature is the group of sharps or flats written at the start of each line, applying to every octave of those notes throughout the piece. It tells you the key. Sharps always appear in the order F, C, G, D, A, E, B (mnemonic "Father Charles Goes Down And Ends Battle"); flats appear in the reverse order, B, E, A, D, G, C, F.

chunks.title: Fundamentals — pitch, intervals, the staff
chunks.heading: The circle of fifths
chunks.text:
## The circle of fifths
The circle of fifths arranges keys by ascending perfect fifths, each adding one sharp: C (0), G (1#), D (2#), A (3#), E (4#), B (5#), F# (6#). Going the other way by fifths (descending, or ascending fourths) adds flats: C (0), F (1b), Bb (2b), Eb (3b), Ab (4b), Db (5b), Gb (6b). It is the map of how keys relate: neighboring keys differ by only one accidental and sound closely related, which is why music often modulates to the key a fifth away.

chunks.title: Fundamentals — pitch, intervals, the staff
chunks.heading: Finding a major key from its signature
chunks.text:
## Finding a major key from its signature
For sharp keys, the major key is one semitone above the last sharp: with F#-C#-G# the last sharp is G#, so the key is A major. For flat keys, the major key is the second-to-last flat: with Bb-Eb-Ab the second-to-last is Eb, so the key is Eb major. (F major, one flat, is the exception you simply memorize.)

chunks.title: Notation in this app — ABC and chord charts
chunks.heading: What this app engraves
chunks.text:
## What this app engraves
This app writes engraved staves from ABC notation in its Score cells, and renders text chord charts in its Cifra (chord-chart) cells. When a learner asks how to write something on the staff here, answer in ABC; when they ask about a chord chart, answer in plain chord-over-lyric text.

chunks.title: Notation in this app — ABC and chord charts
chunks.heading: ABC header fields
chunks.text:
## ABC header fields
An ABC tune starts with header fields, one per line: X: the tune number (X:1), T: the title, C: the composer, M: the meter (M:4/4, or M:C for common time), L: the default note length (L:1/4 means a bare letter is a quarter note; L:1/8 an eighth), Q: the tempo (Q:1/4=120), and K: the key, which also ends the header (K:C, K:Gm, K:D). Everything after K: is music.

chunks.title: Notation in this app — ABC and chord charts
chunks.heading: ABC pitches and octaves
chunks.text:
## ABC pitches and octaves
Letters C D E F G A B are the notes of the octave starting at middle C. Lowercase c d e f g a b are the octave above. A comma lowers an octave (C, is below middle C) and an apostrophe raises one (c' is high). Accidentals go before the note: ^ is sharp (^F = F#), _ is flat (_B = Bb), = is natural. The key signature applies automatically, so you only mark accidentals that depart from it.

chunks.title: Notation in this app — ABC and chord charts
chunks.heading: ABC durations and rests
chunks.text:
## ABC durations and rests
With the default length set by L:, a number multiplies and a slash divides: C2 is twice the default length, C/ or C/2 is half, C3/2 is dotted. A rest is z (visible) or x (invisible); Z is a multi-measure rest. So in L:1/8, "C2 D2 E4" is two eighths' worth, two eighths' worth, then a half-note's worth.

chunks.title: Notation in this app — ABC and chord charts
chunks.heading: ABC barlines and repeats
chunks.text:
## ABC barlines and repeats
A single barline is |, a final barline |], a double barline ||. Repeats use |: to start and :| to end; first and second endings are written [1 and [2 after the repeat. Example: |: C D E F :| repeats that bar.

chunks.title: Notation in this app — ABC and chord charts
chunks.heading: A minimal ABC example
chunks.text:
## A minimal ABC example
A full simple tune:
X:1
T:C major scale
M:4/4
L:1/4
K:C
C D E F | G A B c |
This engraves an ascending C major scale across two bars.

chunks.title: Notation in this app — ABC and chord charts
chunks.heading: ABC chords, ties, and lyrics
chunks.text:
## ABC chords, ties, and lyrics
Notes played together (a chord) go in square brackets: [CEG] is a C major triad. A tie is a hyphen between two notes of the same pitch (C- C). Chord symbols above the staff go in double quotes before the note: "C"C "G7"G. Lyrics go on a w: line below the music, with syllables aligned to notes: w: Twin-kle twin-kle.

chunks.title: Notation in this app — ABC and chord charts
chunks.heading: Chord charts (Cifra cells)
chunks.text:
## Chord charts (Cifra cells)
A chord chart is plain text with chord names placed above the words, the format used on lead sheets and songbook/"cifra" sites. Write the chord names on their own line directly above the lyric line they apply to, lining up each chord over the syllable where it changes. The app reads standard chord symbols (C, Am, G7, Dsus4, F/C) and can transpose the whole chart up or down by semitones, so you write it once in any key.

chunks.title: Progressions, cadences, and harmonic function
chunks.heading: Diatonic triads of a major key
chunks.text:
## Diatonic triads of a major key
Building a triad on each degree of a major scale, using only notes in the key, gives a fixed pattern of qualities, labeled with Roman numerals (uppercase = major, lowercase = minor, ° = diminished): I, ii, iii, IV, V, vi, vii°. In C major: C, Dm, Em, F, G, Am, B°. The uppercase/lowercase tells you the chord quality at a glance.

chunks.title: Progressions, cadences, and harmonic function
chunks.heading: Diatonic triads of a minor key
chunks.text:
## Diatonic triads of a minor key
In natural minor the pattern is i, ii°, III, iv, v, VI, VII. In A minor: Am, B°, C, Dm, Em, F, G. In practice the 7th is often raised (harmonic minor) so the dominant becomes major (V) with a leading tone, giving the strong V–i pull; this also turns vii into a diminished vii°.

chunks.title: Progressions, cadences, and harmonic function
chunks.heading: Harmonic function
chunks.text:
## Harmonic function
Diatonic chords group into three functions that explain how progressions move:
- Tonic (T): I and vi (and iii) — home, rest.
- Predominant / subdominant (PD): IV and ii — departure, setting up the dominant.
- Dominant (D): V and vii° — maximum tension, pulling back to tonic.
The basic cycle is Tonic → Predominant → Dominant → Tonic. Most common progressions are variations on that journey.

chunks.title: Progressions, cadences, and harmonic function
chunks.heading: Cadences
chunks.text:
## Cadences
A cadence is a harmonic punctuation mark at the end of a phrase:
- Perfect authentic cadence (PAC): V–I with both chords in root position and the tonic in the top voice. The strongest, most final close.
- Imperfect authentic cadence (IAC): V–I that is weaker (an inversion, or not the tonic on top).
- Half cadence: a phrase ending on V — it sounds unfinished, like a comma, expecting more.
- Plagal cadence: IV–I, the "Amen" cadence; gentle and conclusive.
- Deceptive cadence: V–vi instead of the expected V–I; the resolution is dodged, prolonging the music.

chunks.title: Progressions, cadences, and harmonic function
chunks.heading: Common progressions
chunks.text:
## Common progressions
- I–IV–V(–I): the backbone of countless folk, blues, and rock songs.
- I–V–vi–IV: the "four-chord" pop progression behind a huge number of hits.
- ii–V–I: the fundamental jazz cadence; in C major that is Dm7–G7–Cmaj7.
- I–vi–IV–V: the "50s" doo-wop progression.
- vi–IV–I–V: another hugely popular pop rotation of the same chords.

chunks.title: Progressions, cadences, and harmonic function
chunks.heading: The twelve-bar blues
chunks.text:
## The twelve-bar blues
The twelve-bar blues is a 12-measure form built on I, IV, and V (often as dominant 7th chords). A standard layout by bar: I-I-I-I / IV-IV-I-I / V-IV-I-V. The final V (the "turnaround") leads back to the top. It underpins blues, early rock and roll, and much of jazz.

chunks.title: Progressions, cadences, and harmonic function
chunks.heading: Secondary dominants
chunks.text:
## Secondary dominants
A secondary dominant is a dominant chord borrowed from another key to tonicize (temporarily point at) a chord other than the tonic. It is written V/x — "V of x." For example V/V in C major is D7, which resolves to G (the V), making that arrival feel stronger. Secondary dominants add chromatic color and momentum without leaving the key for long.

chunks.title: Progressions, cadences, and harmonic function
chunks.heading: Borrowed chords and modal mixture
chunks.text:
## Borrowed chords and modal mixture
Modal mixture (borrowing) takes chords from the parallel key — usually borrowing minor-key chords into a major key. Common examples in major are the minor iv, the bVII, and the bVI. Borrowing darkens or warms a passage while keeping the same tonic, and is everywhere in pop, film, and rock harmony.

chunks.title: Rhythm and meter
chunks.heading: Beat, meter, and time signatures
chunks.text:
## Beat, meter, and time signatures
The beat is the steady pulse you tap your foot to. Meter groups beats into repeating bars. A time signature shows this with two numbers: the top is how many beats per bar, the bottom is which note value gets one beat (4 = quarter note, 8 = eighth note, 2 = half note). So 4/4 is four quarter-note beats per bar, 3/4 is three.

chunks.title: Rhythm and meter
chunks.heading: Simple vs compound meter
chunks.text:
## Simple vs compound meter
In simple meter each beat divides into two (4/4, 3/4, 2/4). In compound meter each beat divides into three, and the time signature's top number is a multiple of three counting the divisions: 6/8 is two beats of three eighth-notes each, 9/8 is three such beats, 12/8 is four. So 6/8 feels like two big "dotted-quarter" beats, not six even ones. Common signatures: 4/4 (common time), 3/4 (waltz), 2/4 (march), 6/8 (compound duple), 12/8 (slow blues/ballad).

chunks.title: Rhythm and meter
chunks.heading: Note durations
chunks.text:
## Note durations
Each note value is half the length of the one before it: whole note (4 beats in 4/4), half note (2), quarter note (1), eighth note (½), sixteenth note (¼), thirty-second (⅛). Rests mirror these durations exactly but mean silence. Beaming joins the flags of eighth and shorter notes into groups that show the beat, making rhythm easier to read.

chunks.title: Rhythm and meter
chunks.heading: Dots and ties
chunks.text:
## Dots and ties
A dot after a note adds half its value again: a dotted half note = half + quarter = 3 beats; a dotted quarter = quarter + eighth = 1½ beats. A second dot adds half of the first dot's value. A tie connects two notes of the same pitch into one sustained sound, used to hold a note across a barline or to make durations a single value can't write.

chunks.title: Rhythm and meter
chunks.heading: Tuplets and triplets
chunks.text:
## Tuplets and triplets
A tuplet squeezes an "irregular" number of notes into the time of a normal grouping. The most common is the triplet: three notes in the time of two of the same value (an eighth-note triplet fills one quarter-note beat with three even notes). Duplets and quadruplets do the reverse in compound meter; quintuplets fit five, and so on.

chunks.title: Rhythm and meter
chunks.heading: Syncopation
chunks.text:
## Syncopation
Syncopation places accents off the expected strong beats — on weak beats or between beats (the offbeats) — creating a sense of push, groove, or surprise. Ties, rests on strong beats, and accented upbeats all produce syncopation. It is central to jazz, funk, Latin, and most popular music.

chunks.title: Rhythm and meter
chunks.heading: Swing
chunks.text:
## Swing
Swing is a performance feel where pairs of eighth notes are played long-short rather than evenly, roughly as the first two notes of a triplet. It is usually written as plain eighth notes with a "swing" instruction rather than spelled out. Swing gives jazz and blues their characteristic lilt.

chunks.title: Rhythm and meter
chunks.heading: Tempo
chunks.text:
## Tempo
Tempo is the speed of the beat, measured in beats per minute (BPM). Traditional Italian terms give approximate ranges: Largo (very slow, ~40–60), Adagio (slow, ~66–76), Andante (walking, ~76–108), Moderato (moderate, ~108–120), Allegro (fast, ~120–168), Presto (very fast, ~168–200). A metronome marking like ♩ = 120 states it exactly: 120 quarter-note beats per minute.

chunks.title: Scales and modes
chunks.heading: The major scale
chunks.text:
## The major scale
A major scale follows the step pattern whole-whole-half-whole-whole-whole-half (W W H W W W H). Starting on C gives all white keys: C D E F G A B C. The half steps fall between the 3rd–4th and 7th–8th degrees. This pattern is the reference every other scale and mode is measured against.

chunks.title: Scales and modes
chunks.heading: Scale degree names
chunks.text:
## Scale degree names
Each degree of a major or minor scale has a function name: 1 tonic, 2 supertonic, 3 mediant, 4 subdominant, 5 dominant, 6 submediant, 7 leading tone (when a semitone below the tonic) or subtonic (when a whole tone below, as in natural minor). The tonic is home; the dominant (5) creates the strongest pull back to it; the leading tone (7) "leans" up into the tonic.

chunks.title: Scales and modes
chunks.heading: Natural minor scale
chunks.text:
## Natural minor scale
The natural minor scale follows W H W W H W W. A natural minor is all white keys: A B C D E F G A. Compared with the major scale of the same starting note (parallel major), natural minor has a lowered 3rd, 6th, and 7th (b3, b6, b7), which gives it its darker sound.

chunks.title: Scales and modes
chunks.heading: Harmonic and melodic minor
chunks.text:
## Harmonic and melodic minor
Natural minor lacks a leading tone, weakening the pull to the tonic, so two variants raise the 7th. Harmonic minor raises the 7th by a semitone (A B C D E F G# A), creating a strong leading tone but an awkward augmented-2nd gap between the 6th and 7th. Melodic minor raises both the 6th and 7th ascending (A B C D E F# G# A) to smooth that gap, and traditionally reverts to natural minor descending.

chunks.title: Scales and modes
chunks.heading: Relative major and minor
chunks.text:
## Relative major and minor
Every major key shares its key signature with a minor key a minor third below — its relative minor, built on the 6th degree of the major. C major and A minor both have no sharps or flats; G major and E minor both have one sharp. They use the same notes but treat a different note as home.

chunks.title: Scales and modes
chunks.heading: Parallel major and minor
chunks.text:
## Parallel major and minor
Parallel keys share the same tonic but differ in quality: C major and C minor. Switching between them (borrowing chords or shifting mode) is a common expressive device. Unlike relatives, parallels have different key signatures (C major 0 accidentals, C minor 3 flats).

chunks.title: Scales and modes
chunks.heading: The modes
chunks.text:
## The modes
The seven modes are the major scale started on each of its seven degrees, each with a distinct character:
- Ionian (1st degree) = the major scale.
- Dorian (2nd) = minor with a raised 6th; jazzy, folk.
- Phrygian (3rd) = minor with a lowered 2nd; Spanish, dark.
- Lydian (4th) = major with a raised 4th; bright, dreamy.
- Mixolydian (5th) = major with a lowered 7th; bluesy, rock.
- Aeolian (6th) = the natural minor scale.
- Locrian (7th) = diminished, with a lowered 2nd and 5th; unstable, rare.
The white-key modes are C Ionian, D Dorian, E Phrygian, F Lydian, G Mixolydian, A Aeolian, B Locrian.

chunks.title: Scales and modes
chunks.heading: Pentatonic scales
chunks.text:
## Pentatonic scales
A pentatonic scale has five notes per octave. The major pentatonic is the major scale without its 4th and 7th (C D E G A) — no semitones, so nothing clashes, which makes it forgiving for improvising. The minor pentatonic is the natural minor without its 2nd and 6th (A C D E G); it is the backbone of rock, blues, and pop soloing.

chunks.title: Scales and modes
chunks.heading: The blues scale
chunks.text:
## The blues scale
The blues scale is the minor pentatonic plus an added chromatic "blue note," the flat 5th: A C D Eb E G. That extra note, used as a passing tone, gives blues, rock, and jazz their characteristic bent, expressive sound.

chunks.title: Scales and modes
chunks.heading: Chromatic and whole-tone scales
chunks.text:
## Chromatic and whole-tone scales
The chromatic scale uses all twelve semitones in order and has no tonic of its own; it supplies passing notes and tension. The whole-tone scale divides the octave into six equal whole steps (C D E F# G# A#), sounds ambiguous and "floating" because no note feels like home, and is associated with Impressionist composers like Debussy.
